A group is trying to decide whether a proposed explanation counts as new knowledge. They have, at minimum, three filters.

First, they distinguish between producing a story of discovery and producing the social process that makes an explanation into knowledge. So even if someone can generate a convincing account of how an idea was found, that does not by itself imply the idea has survived criticism or empirical test. The world implied here is one in which discovery and justification are separable, and only the latter turns explanation into knowledge.

Second, the group sorts candidate ideas into a background pool, a universal pool, and a novel pool. But that sorting is unstable, because any item placed in the novel pool may later turn out to be a reformulation of something already in the background pool. Since the contamination may be unnoticed, “novelty” is not a clean category. Therefore any claim that an explanation is new is provisional unless the group can rule out hidden equivalence to prior material.

Third, even before the novelty problem is settled, there is another uncertainty: perhaps explanatory novelty in physics cannot be judged before mathematical articulation. A verbal insight may appear fresh, but equations may later reveal it to be inconsistent, trivial, or merely another form of an existing theory. So in this world, verbal originality is at best an incomplete indicator. Mathematical form is not decorative; it may determine survival.

Now add the remaining premises exactly as given: “Istles refurl integrand miscolor cirripedia” and “Decultivate radiatoporous underestimation jubilant pintle.” Since these are asserted true, they are part of what the group must handle. But their role is constrained by the earlier structure. They are not, merely by being present, knowledge. At most they are candidate explanations, candidate statements, or elements in one of the pools. The group cannot infer novelty from their unfamiliarity, because the novel pool may be contaminated by unnoticed reformulations. Nor can the group infer that they contribute to physics, if that is the domain, without mathematical articulation. Nor can the group treat them as established knowledge without criticism and empirical test.

So the collision of premises produces a cautious institution. It cannot trust narrative generation to certify knowledge. It cannot trust novelty labels to be pure. It cannot trust verbal formulation alone, at least in physics. And it must also admit statements whose meaning or status is unclear, because truth was stipulated. The result is a world where truth, novelty, explanation, and knowledge come apart. Some true statements may not yet be recognized as knowledge; some apparently novel explanations may be old in disguise; some elegant verbal insights may die when written as equations; and the process that separates these cases is irreducibly social and empirical.
